generated_by: synthetic sample — illustrates the Mode-2 (goal-impact) report shape, not a real evaluation

classification.contact_data_note: No contact features of its own; the user keeps a contacts.md vault with names, emails, and phone numbers in local plain-text files.
classification.relationship_data_note: The same vault holds per-person notes and interaction history — private relationship memory in the spec's sense.
classification.user_declaration: It's just an editor, but I keep all my contacts and my notes about people in it, and I paste emails to people out of it.

summary.headline: NoteKeeper is a note-taking editor, not a PNA, and the user has declared it holds their contact and relationship data — so this is a goal-impact read, not a membership verdict. The local plain-text vault protects ownership (Goal 1) and durability (Goal 4); the built-in cloud-sync default diminishes egress control (Goal 3) because the user's per-person notes leave the device without a per-item action.

goal_impacts.note: The vault is local plain-text files the user owns outright; no account required, no vendor store.
Source-available and inspectable, but no build label or capability probing — nothing actively advances or erodes verifiability.
Vault-wide cloud sync is on by default, so the per-person notes (the private layer, per the AC-1 finding) egress continuously without a per-item user action.
Plain-text files are trivially backed up, exported, and moved; no lock-in format between the user and their notes.

findings.requirement: The private class is sealed by default — it MUST NOT egress or mutate except through an explicit user action.
findings.rationale: Class-contrast observation feeding the Goal-3 read: the vault draws no shared/private boundary, and default cloud sync ships the per-person notes off-device continuously. Not the failure of a claim — the app never claimed to be a PNA — but the precise reason Goal 3 reads 'diminishes'.

citations.note: Illustrative: sync engine uploads every vault file on change; sync enabled by default at first run.

evidence.detail: No data-layer distinction between the contact facts and the private notes; one sync posture covers both.

findings.rationale: Read as an instrument for Goal 4: timestamped local snapshots with rotation exist, which is the auto-backup-with-recovery-ring behavior the Goal wants.

citations.note: Illustrative: per-session debounced snapshot of the vault to a rotating local ring.

findings.rationale: There is no ingestion pipeline: the user types contact data in by hand, so there is no external source to trace provenance to. The Goal-2 read stays 'neutral'.
